REPORT ADDENDUM: RAG DATABASE REQUIREMENTS, PGEDGE CANDIDACY, AND VECTOR STORAGE INTEGRATION

This addendum exhaustively documents the database requirements for integrating a Small Language Model (SLM) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) into the existing decentralized social media stack, strictly evaluating pgEdge as a candidate and recommending the optimal vector database for production-scale RAG. All details are grounded in the previously established architecture using Willow, Meadowcap, iroh, did:peer, PostgreSQL/pgEdge, ScyllaDB, and the event bus. No philosophical commentary or non-recommended products are included.

SECTION 1: RAG DATABASE REQUIREMENTS IN THIS STACK

A RAG database must satisfy five core requirements to function correctly within this architecture. First, it must store high-dimensional vector embeddings alongside structured metadata including author_did, namespace_id, subspace_id, path, timestamp, content_type, and credential_requirements. Second, it must support approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) indexing with low-latency similarity search at scale. Third, it must enable precise metadata filtering to enforce access control during retrieval, ensuring that only content authorized by Meadowcap capabilities and user credentials is returned. Fourth, it must support hybrid search that combines vector similarity with keyword or full-text matching for precision. Fifth, it must integrate cleanly with the existing event-driven ingestion pipeline, allowing asynchronous vectorization and indexing without blocking P2P sync or social velocity layers.

SECTION 2: PGEDGE AS A RAG CANDIDATE

pgEdge is a viable candidate for RAG workloads when extended with the pgvector extension. pgvector adds native vector data types, distance operators (L2, cosine, inner product), and indexing algorithms to PostgreSQL. When deployed across a pgEdge multi-master cluster, it provides a unified relational and vector store that aligns with the existing truth-index layer.

Implementation requires installing pgvector on each pgEdge node and configuring HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) indexing for production ANN performance. HNSW outperforms IVFFlat for low-latency queries, supports incremental index building, and handles dynamic inserts efficiently. The index must be created with explicit distance metric selection matching the embedding model's output space, typically cosine for text embeddings. Memory allocation for HNSW must be tuned via maintenance_work_mem and max_parallel_workers to prevent index build stalls. For large-scale deployments, vector tables should be partitioned by time or namespace_id using PostgreSQL declarative partitioning to isolate index scope and reduce search surface.

pgEdge's multi-master replication introduces specific considerations for RAG. Vector inserts are straightforward, but concurrent updates to the same document's embeddings can cause write conflicts. Conflict resolution should be configured to prefer latest-writer or application-defined merge logic, with explicit idempotent upserts using (author_did, namespace_id, path) as the natural key. Read scaling for vector queries should route to read replicas or leverage pgEdge's distributed query routing to avoid saturating write nodes. Hybrid search combines pgvector distance operators with PostgreSQL's tsvector full-text search using a single query that ranks by vector distance and filters by metadata, but this requires careful index selection to avoid sequential scans on large partitions.

pgEdge's candidacy is strongest when RAG workload volume is moderate, when operational simplicity favors a single database cluster, and when metadata filtering is complex enough to benefit from full SQL expressiveness. It becomes strained when vector search exceeds tens of millions of embeddings, when sub-50ms p99 retrieval is mandatory, or when multi-master replication overhead degrades ANN index performance. In those cases, a dedicated vector database is required alongside pgEdge.

SECTION 3: RECOMMENDED DEDICATED VECTOR DATABASE: QDRANT

Qdrant is the recommended vector database for high-scale RAG in this stack. It is written in Rust, aligns with the stack's local-first and self-hosted ethos, and provides production-grade ANN performance with fine-grained payload filtering. Qdrant stores vectors and associated JSON payloads in a single structure, enabling exact metadata filtering during retrieval without secondary indexes. It supports HNSW and PQ (Product Quantization) indexing, with automatic optimization for memory and latency trade-offs.

The official qdrant-client Rust crate provides async, tokio-native connectivity with gRPC and HTTP APIs, prepared queries, and batch upserts. Collections should be configured with distance metric matching the embedding model, typically Cosine, and indexing parameters tuned for the expected dataset size. HNSW parameters m and ef_construct should be set to balance recall and index build time. Payload indexes must be created explicitly for fields used in filtering, such as did:peer, namespace_id, credential_requirements, and timestamp, to ensure filter-then-search execution rather than post-filtering, which degrades performance.

Qdrant scales horizontally via sharding and replication, with explicit consistency controls for write acknowledgments. It supports snapshotting and point-in-time recovery, and integrates cleanly with Kubernetes via the official operator. For RAG ingestion, Qdrant should receive events from the same event bus that feeds pgEdge and ScyllaDB, with a dedicated consumer group running the embedding pipeline. This decouples vectorization from sync and social velocity layers, preserving the P2P stack's guarantees.

SECTION 4: RAG PIPELINE INTEGRATION WITH EXISTING STACK

The RAG ingestion pipeline operates as a parallel consumer of the event bus. When Willow sync verifies a did:peer signature and evaluates a Meadowcap capability, it publishes a structured event to the bus. The RAG consumer extracts the text payload, applies chunking logic optimized for the target SLM's context window (typically 300-500 token chunks with 50-token overlap), generates embeddings using a local or hosted embedding model, and upserts the vectors to Qdrant or pgvector. Each chunk retains metadata mapping back to the original entry: author_did, namespace_id, subspace_id, path, timestamp, capability_scope, and content_hash. Chunks are stored with a deterministic ID derived from content_hash and chunk_index to enable idempotent updates during sync repairs.

Query execution begins when the SLM receives a user prompt. The RAG retriever first evaluates the user's active credentials and Meadowcap capabilities against pgEdge to determine authorized namespaces, paths, and content scopes. The retriever then constructs a vector search query targeting only authorized partitions, applying metadata filters for credential_requirements, namespace bounds, and timestamp ranges. Qdrant or pgvector returns ranked chunks with similarity scores. The retriever applies a relevance threshold, deduplicates overlapping chunks, and passes the curated context to the SLM for response generation. All retrieval logic runs in the application layer; the vector database never evaluates access control, only enforces payload filters provided by the application.

SECTION 5: ACCESS CONTROL AND PRIVACY IN RAG RETRIEVAL

RAG must strictly respect the private/personal distinction and capability-based access model. Only entries with public or delegated read capabilities are eligible for embedding and vector storage. Encrypted payloads are never processed, chunked, or indexed. Credential gating occurs at query time: the application resolves the user's VC cache from pgEdge, maps it to authorized Areas, and injects those constraints into the vector search filter. This ensures that retrieval respects social context without requiring the vector database to understand cryptographic proofs or DID resolution.

If a capability is revoked or a credential expires, the application must trigger metadata updates in the vector store to invalidate affected chunks. This is achieved by upserting chunks with updated credential_requirements or soft-deletion flags, then running periodic vacuum or compaction to remove stale vectors from active index space. Tombstone tracking in pgEdge drives invalidation jobs that update vector metadata in batches to avoid index thrashing.

SECTION 6: EMBEDDING PIPELINE AND OPERATIONAL CONFIGURATION

The embedding pipeline must be stateless, horizontally scalable, and integrated with the event bus. It should use a fixed embedding model version to ensure vector space consistency across deployments. Model weights should be cached locally or served via a dedicated inference endpoint with connection pooling and batch inference support. Chunking must preserve metadata boundaries, avoid splitting structured content (e.g., code blocks, tables), and include a content_hash field for idempotent upserts.

Operational configuration for Qdrant includes setting consistent write acknowledgments (quorum or all for critical indexing), enabling snapshot backups on a separate volume, and monitoring index build latency, search p99, and payload filter hit rates. For pgvector, configuration includes tuning HNSW ef_search at query time based on acceptable recall/latency trade-offs, maintaining index statistics via ANALYZE, and partitioning vector tables by month or namespace to isolate index scope. Both systems require explicit monitoring of embedding drift; if the model version changes, a full re-embedding pipeline must run alongside the existing index until migration completes.

SECTION 7: FALLBACK AND DEGRADATION STRATEGY

The RAG layer must degrade gracefully without impacting core sync or social features. If Qdrant or pgvector becomes unavailable, the application falls back to keyword-only search using pgEdge's tsvector indexes, returning exact matches without semantic ranking. If the embedding pipeline stalls, new content continues to sync and index relationally; vectorization resumes asynchronously with catch-up logic driven by event bus offset tracking. If the SLM inference endpoint fails, the application returns a structured template response with direct links to relevant documents, preserving functionality while bypassing generation. All fallback paths must log retrieval state for auditability and resume vector indexing without data loss.

SECTION 8: FINAL ARCHITECTURAL INVARIANTS FOR RAG

Cryptographic truth and authorship remain exclusively in the Willow/Meadowcap/did:peer layer. The vector database stores only derived embeddings and metadata, never signing keys or capability grants. RAG retrieval is always scoped by capability-evaluating application logic, never by the vector store itself. Vector ingestion is strictly asynchronous, driven by the same event bus that feeds pgEdge and ScyllaDB, ensuring sync performance is never blocked by embedding latency. All vector operations are idempotent, partitioned, and filter-ready to support credential-gated, offline-resilient RAG at scale.

This addendum contains all product recommendations, configuration details, integration patterns, and operational guardrails required to implement RAG with an SLM in the existing stack. It is structured for direct ingestion by another AI agent, with exhaustive technical specificity and no extraneous content.
